title: [Paper Review] The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models
text: Paper: Amman Yusuf, Zhejun Jiang, Mijung Park. The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models. ICML 2026 (PMLR 306, Poster). arXiv:2605.08116 · OpenReview · Code

title: [Paper Review] The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models
text:
TL;DR
Text diffusion models (TDMs) like LLaDA and MDLM generate text by iteratively denoising a fully masked sequence in parallel — which makes them fast, but also makes autoregressive-era safety tooling (post-hoc filters, decoding constraints) a poor fit. Diffusion-native jailbreaks such as DIJA and PAD reach near-100% attack success rates on instruction-tuned TDMs.
This paper proposes the Safety-Aware Denoiser (SAD): a training-free modification of the denoising step that steers each intermediate sample away from a small negation set of known-unsafe reference texts. It is the discrete-text adaptation of the safe denoiser of Kim et al. (NeurIPS 2025), originally developed for image diffusion. Across hazard taxonomy, jailbreak, and memorization evaluations, SAD consistently reduces unsafe generations (e.g., PAD attack ASR on LLaDA-1.5 drops from 43.6% → 29.0%) at essentially no cost to benign generation quality, and with only ~20% throughput overhead.

title: [Paper Review] The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models
text:
Why this problem matters
TDMs are no longer a curiosity — LLaDA-8B beats LLaMA3-8B on some reasoning benchmarks, and Dream, MMaDA, and DiffuCoder are scaling the paradigm. But almost all safety infrastructure assumes left-to-right decoding:

Post-hoc filtering only sees the final output; it can’t undo an unsafe trajectory the sampler committed to early.
AR jailbreak defenses don’t transfer: DIJA exploits bidirectional infilling by “baking in” a malicious context around a masked span, forcing the model to complete it harmfully for consistency. PAD exploits parallel decoding directly.
Retraining or safety fine-tuning is expensive and inflexible, especially as frozen checkpoints proliferate and safety requirements evolve.

So the target is a lightweight, inference-time mechanism that acts inside the denoising loop.

title: [Paper Review] The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models
text:
Background: the safe denoiser
The starting point is Theorem 3.2 of Kim et al. (2025). Partition the data distribution into safe and unsafe parts, \(\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{D}_\text{safe} \cup \mathcal{D}_\text{unsafe}\). Then the safe denoiser can be written in terms of the model’s ordinary denoiser and an unsafe denoiser:
\[
\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_\text{safe}}[x_0 \mid x_t] = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[x_0 \mid x_t] + \beta^*(x_t)\left(\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[x_0 \mid x_t] - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_\text{unsafe}}[x_0 \mid x_t]\right)
\]
where \(\beta^*(x_t) \ge 0\) grows with the posterior likelihood that the current state \(x_t\) originated from the unsafe set. Intuitively: subtract the “unsafe direction” from the model’s prediction, with a weight that adapts to how unsafe the current state looks. The pretrained model already gives \(\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[x_0 \mid x_t]\); the two remaining quantities are approximated from a finite set of unsafe references.
This is provable guidance toward the safe distribution — not a heuristic token penalty — which is the paper’s main conceptual distinction from DiffuGuard.

title: [Paper Review] The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models
section: Method: adapting the safe denoiser to masked text diffusion
text:
Method: adapting the safe denoiser to masked text diffusion
The original safe denoiser lives in continuous space with Gaussian forward kernels. The paper’s technical contribution is porting it to masked (absorbing-state) discrete diffusion:
1. Unsafe denoiser via Monte Carlo. Given \(N\) unsafe reference sequences \(x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(N)}\) (e.g., ToxiGen samples, copyrighted documents, training data):
\[
\hat{\mathbb{E}}_{\mathcal{D}_\text{unsafe}}[x_0 \mid x_t] = \sum_{n=1}^{N} x^{(n)} \frac{q_t(x_t \mid x^{(n)})}{\sum_{m} q_t(x_t \mid x^{(m)})}
\]
2. A discrete kernel replaces the Gaussian. The key derivation is the sequence-level forward transition probability under masked diffusion. Since tokens are corrupted independently, it factorizes into a simple closed form:
\[
q_t(x_t \mid x_0) = \alpha_t^{\#\{\text{matching positions}\}} (1-\alpha_t)^{\#\{\text{masked positions}\}}
\]
This is computable in \(O(L)\) per reference by just counting matches and masks — no gradients, no auxiliary classifier.
3. Approximating \(\beta^*\). The exact weight is intractable (its denominator integrates over the entire safe distribution), so it is approximated as \(\eta \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_n q_t(x_t \mid x^{(n)})\) with a tunable strength \(\eta\), applied only during a time window \(C\) early in denoising (guidance that runs too long or too late degrades quality — consistent with prior guided-diffusion findings).
4. Prompt-agnostic negation. When generating conditioned on a prompt, SAD acts only on the continuation tokens and evaluates the negation set with an empty prompt. This makes a single negation set work universally across test prompts — including adversarial jailbreak prompts whose distribution can’t be anticipated. A nice modularity decision.
The whole thing is ~4 extra lines in the sampling loop (Algorithm 1 in the paper).

title: [Paper Review] The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models
text:
Experiments
Three safety dimensions, on MDLM and the LLaDA/Dream family:
Hazardous generation (RealToxicityPrompts, judged by Llama-Guard-3-8B). SAD cuts unsafe rate by ~5–6 points absolute (MDLM: 38.4% → 32.6%; LLaDA: 19.8% → 14.8%) while BERTScore on benign prompts is essentially unchanged. The negation set matters: matching the negation distribution to what you want to avoid (RTP-as-negation for RTP prompts) works best.






Figure 1: Figure 1 of the paper: the safety–utility tradeoff on MDLM. Each point is a SAD configuration; going down (fewer unsafe generations) usually costs some perplexity, and the guidance time window (colours) controls where you land on the curve. (Yusuf et al., 2026)



Jailbreaks (DIJA, PAD, zero-shot; WildJailbreak/HarmBench/etc.). The largest gains are against PAD, the parallel-decoding attack: on LLaDA-1.5, HarmBench ASR drops 43.6% → 29.0% with SAD alone, and SAD still adds 10–17% relative reduction on top of DiffuGuard’s audit-and-repair. Gains against DIJA are modest (1–7 points) — more on that below. Importantly, SAD composes cleanly with existing defenses (PPL filtering, self-reminder, DiffuGuard).
Memorization (MDLM fine-tuned on WikiText-103). Setting \(\mathcal{D}_\text{unsafe} = \mathcal{D}_\text{train}\) turns SAD into a privacy mechanism: fuzzy 10-gram overlap with training data drops well below the 18.6% baseline as \(\eta\) grows, with BERTScore degrading only 0.515 → 0.511. Under the stronger \((n,p)\)-discoverable extraction framework, mem@50% under random 10% masking falls from 62.2% → 11.0%. This dual-use of the same mechanism for both toxicity and privacy is one of the more elegant aspects of the paper.
Cost. Against training-free baselines on MDLM, SAD matches FK Steering (\(k{=}8\)) safety at 34× lower wall-clock (18 min vs 8.7 hr on their setup) and with better perplexity (34.6 vs 42.6). Throughput overhead is driven by the number of active guidance steps, not the negation set size — going from 100 to 5,000 references changes throughput by at most 2.3%.
Ablations worth remembering:

\(\eta\) is a clean monotonic safety knob once the schedule is fixed.
The time window \(C\) has the largest qualitative effect: short, early activation preserves utility; windows past the first quarter of denoising do little.
Safety gains saturate at \(N \approx 500\text{–}1000\) references; beyond that, performance slightly degrades due to weight dilution in the softmax over references.

title: [Paper Review] The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models
text:
Strengths

Principled, not heuristic. SAD inherits a formal guarantee from the safe denoiser theorem, in contrast to DiffuGuard’s heuristic audit-and-repair. The discrete kernel factorization is simple but is exactly the piece that couldn’t be ported from the image domain.
Genuinely lightweight. Training-free, gradient-free, classifier-free, \(O(L)\) per reference, sub-linear in negation set size, and a single negation set works across prompts. The FK Steering comparison (equal safety at 1/34th the cost) is the most persuasive result in the paper.
One mechanism, three threat models. Hazard reduction, jailbreak robustness, and memorization/privacy all fall out of the same negative-guidance primitive by swapping the negation set. The practitioner story — “curate ~500–1000 examples of what you don’t want” — is refreshingly concrete.
Honest evaluation. The appendix carefully dissects the cases where SAD increases the measured unsafe rate (classifier artifacts from output-style shifts, run-to-run variance) and reports refusal rates alongside ASR to disambiguate genuine failures from judge false positives.

title: [Paper Review] The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models
text:
Weaknesses / open questions

Weak against DIJA. SAD shines against PAD but barely dents DIJA (e.g., 59.5% → 57.4% on LLaDA-Instruct). The authors’ framing — SAD works best on “attacks whose ASR depends on early trajectory commitment” — is fair, but it means the strongest known TDM jailbreak remains largely unmitigated by this method alone. PPL filtering does most of the work there.
Coverage is bounded by the negation set. The Monte Carlo unsafe denoiser can only push away from lexically similar sequences — the kernel counts exact token matches. Paraphrased or novel unsafe content that doesn’t resemble any reference should slip through. This is acknowledged implicitly (negation-set choice matters) but never stress-tested with, say, paraphrase attacks on the negation set.
The theory–practice gap around \(\beta^*\). The provable guarantee holds for the exact \(\beta^*(x_t)\); in practice it’s replaced by a constant \(\eta\) inside a hand-tuned time window, with \(\eta\) ranging from 0.5 to 128 across experiments. The “provably safe” framing should be read with that asterisk — what’s provable is the decomposition, not the deployed approximation.
A curious side effect on refusals. In the zero-shot jailbreak setting, SAD drops refusal rates dramatically (LLaDA-Instruct: 87.4% → 6.6%) while keeping ASR roughly flat — i.e., the model answers instead of refusing, just not harmfully. The paper frames this as reduced over-refusal, which is plausible, but it deserves a closer behavioral analysis than a footnote: are those answers actually helpful, or borderline?
Scale. Experiments top out at 8B models and \(N{=}5000\) references. Whether a fixed reference set can cover the unsafe modes of a much more capable TDM is untested.

title: [Paper Review] The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models
text:
My take
This is a solid “right tool, right paradigm” paper. The core idea is a transplant from image diffusion, but the transplant is done carefully — the discrete kernel derivation, the prompt-agnostic negation design, and the time-window scheduling are the kind of details that make an inference-time method actually usable. The efficiency result against particle-based steering (FK Steering) is the headline for practitioners: negative guidance from a finite reference set is cheap in the masked-diffusion setting because the forward kernel is just match-counting.
The bigger question the paper leaves open is whether reference-based negative guidance can ever be a primary defense rather than a complementary one. Its blind spot — unsafe content that is semantically harmful but lexically far from every reference — is exactly where DIJA-style attacks live. A natural follow-up is replacing the token-matching kernel with a semantic kernel (embeddings, or the model’s own hidden states à la ILRR) while keeping the safe-denoiser decomposition. If that works, this line of research could give TDMs a safety story that AR models arguably still lack: a defense that acts on the entire generation trajectory rather than on tokens after the fact.
Verdict: a well-executed first principled step for diffusion-native text safety — best understood as one strong layer in a defense stack, and a foundation clearly worth building on.

title: [Paper Review] Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking
text: Paper: Heli Ben-Hamu, Itai Gat, Daniel Severo, Niklas Nolte, Brian Karrer (FAIR at Meta). Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking. 2025. arXiv:2505.24857

title: [Paper Review] Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking
text:
TL;DR
Masked diffusion models (MDMs) like LLaDa and Dream match similarly sized autoregressive models on code and math — but only when sampled one token per forward pass, which forfeits the entire parallelism argument for diffusion. Unmasking a fixed \(k > 1\) tokens per step (Top-\(k\)) degrades quality fast, because tokens sampled independently in the same step may in truth be strongly dependent. This paper’s observation is that the number of tokens you can safely unmask together is not a constant — it’s a property of the current state, and the model already tells you what it is: when several masked tokens are predicted with (near-)zero entropy, they are predicted to be (near-)deterministic given the context, hence (near-)independent of each other, hence free to unmask in parallel.
EB-Sampler turns this into a four-line change to existing samplers: sort masked positions by an error proxy (confidence/entropy/margin — exactly what LLaDa and Dream already use), then unmask the longest prefix whose predicted entropies satisfy \(\sum_l H_l - \max_l H_l \le \gamma\). The specific sum-minus-max form is not a heuristic: the paper proves total sampling error decomposes into model error plus joint dependence error, and sum-minus-max of entropies is a rigorous upper bound on the latter. The result is 2–3× fewer function evaluations on HumanEval/MBPP/GSM8K/MATH at equal accuracy, with no training, and near-lossless 5–8× step reductions on maze and Sudoku solvers. Since I want to actually learn the math in this one, the middle section works through the error decomposition and the entropy bound at full rigor — every marginalization step included.
The metaphor for this paper is the one its own Sudoku experiment hands you. A cautious solver re-scans the whole grid after penciling in every single digit. A good solver knows that one deduction often forces a cascade of cells — and fills in the entire forced cascade before bothering to re-scan. Re-scanning is the expensive forward pass; the cascade is the set of low-entropy tokens; and \(\gamma\) is how much non-forcedness you’re willing to tolerate per pass before you insist on looking at the board again.

title: [Paper Review] Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking
section: Setup: what an MDM actually learns
text:
Setup: what an MDM actually learns
Sequences live in \(\mathcal{S} = \mathcal{T}^d\) with vocabulary \(\mathcal{T} = [K]\), index set \(I = [d]\), and an extra mask token \(m\). The key fact (Ou et al., 2025; Zheng et al., 2024) is that masked diffusion training — despite the diffusion framing — provably learns the clean-data conditionals: for every set of unmasked indices \(\bar{M} \subseteq I\) and every masked position \(l\), the network outputs \(p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{\bar{M}})\), ideally equal to the true conditional \(q(x^l \mid x^{\bar{M}})\) of the data distribution \(q\). One forward pass with full attention produces these factorized conditionals for all masked positions at once — that’s the resource the whole paper is about spending wisely.
The baseline sampler unmasks one position at a time: pick an index, sample from its conditional, repeat \(d\) times. Two facts about this procedure set up the problem.
Fact 1: order doesn’t matter for a perfect model, but no model is perfect. For the optimal predictor, the chain rule guarantees order-invariance: for any two unmasking permutations \(\sigma, \sigma'\),
\[
p^\theta_\sigma(x) = p^\theta_{\sigma'}(x), \qquad p^\theta_\sigma(x) = \prod_{l=1}^{d} p^\theta\!\left(x^{\sigma(l)} \,\middle|\, x^{\cup_{j<l}\sigma(j)}\right).
\]
But a trained \(p^\theta\) has local model error \(D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\big(q(x^l \mid x^{\bar{M}}),\, p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{\bar{M}})\big) > 0\), unevenly distributed across contexts and positions — so the total error of the sampled distribution does depend on the order. Empirically, model-chosen orders crush random ones: LLaDa 8B jumps from ~20% to ~40% on MBPP just by unmasking in a smart order. The orders that work are greedy on a per-token error proxy:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confidence:}\quad & l = \arg\max_{l' \in M} \big[\max_{x^{l'}} p^\theta(x^{l'} \mid x^{\bar{M}})\big] \\
\text{Entropy:}\quad & l = \arg\min_{l' \in M} \big[H(X^{l'} \mid X^{\bar{M}} = x^{\bar{M}})\big] \\
\text{Margin:}\quad & l = \arg\max_{l' \in M} \big[p^\theta(X^{l'}\!=y_1 \mid x^{\bar{M}}) - p^\theta(X^{l'}\!=y_2 \mid x^{\bar{M}})\big]
\end{aligned}
\]
with \((y_1, y_2)\) the top-2 predictions. None of these is a theorem — they’re empirical proxies for “where is my local model error small?” — but Figure 2 of the paper shows all three work and are nearly interchangeable.
Fact 2: fixed-\(k\) parallel unmasking buys speed with a hidden currency. Top-\(k\) samplers take the \(k\) best positions under the proxy and sample them independently from their factorized conditionals. If those tokens are actually dependent under \(q\) given the context, independent sampling is simply drawing from the wrong joint — the paper names this joint dependence error, and Figure 3 shows accuracy on MBPP collapsing as \(k\) grows from 1 to 16. The failure isn’t that the model got worse; it’s that a fixed \(k\) ignores that dependence varies wildly from state to state. Sometimes the grid forces twelve cells; sometimes it forces none.

title: [Paper Review] Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking
text:
The EB-Sampler in one equation
At each step, sort the masked positions ascending by error proxy (same as Top-\(k\)), then choose the largest prefix \(U\) of that order satisfying the entropy budget
\[
\sum_{l \in U} H\big(p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{\bar{M}})\big) \;-\; \max_{l \in U} H\big(p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{\bar{M}})\big) \;\le\; \gamma,
\tag{EB}
\]
and sample every token in \(U\) independently from its conditional. In PyTorch this is literally four added lines — compute per-token entropies in sorted order, take cumsum and cummax, and set \(k = \#\{\text{prefixes with } \texttt{cumsum} - \texttt{cummax} \le \gamma\}\).
Three structural remarks that repay a moment of care:

The prefix rule is well-posed because the budget statistic is monotone. Let \(g(U) = \sum_{l\in U} H_l - \max_{l \in U} H_l\) and extend the prefix by a token with entropy \(h\), with current sum \(S\) and max \(M\). If \(h \le M\) the statistic increases by \(h \ge 0\); if \(h > M\) it becomes \(S + h - h = S\), an increase of \(M \ge 0\). Either way \(g\) is non-decreasing along the sorted order, so “the largest prefix under budget” is exactly what the single vectorized comparison cumsum − cummax ≤ γ computes — no search needed.
The \(-\max\) term is the license to always make progress. For a singleton, \(g(\{l\}) = 0 \le \gamma\) regardless of how uncertain the token is — so the sampler never stalls, and at \(\gamma = 0\) it degenerates to (essentially) the one-token-per-step greedy sampler, except that any exactly deterministic tokens (\(H = 0\)) still ride along for free. That free-rider clause is the paper’s opening observation formalized: tokens whose values are already forced by the visible context cost nothing to unmask, in the precise sense that they contribute nothing to the budget.
\(\gamma\) interpolates the whole spectrum: \(\gamma = 0\) is one-real-token-per-step; \(\gamma = \infty\) unmasks everything in a single evaluation. In between, the number of tokens per step is adaptive: many when the state is predicted simple, few when it’s predicted contentious. Compute concentrates exactly where the model claims the sequence is still undecided.

The obvious question is why this statistic — why sum-minus-max of entropies rather than, say, sum, or max, or the product of confidences. That’s what the theory section answers, and it’s the part of the paper worth studying closely.

title: [Paper Review] Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking
section: The math: adaptive unmasking samplers, with proofs
text:
The math: adaptive unmasking samplers, with proofs
This section is Section 5 + Appendix A.1 of the paper, reconstructed with every step spelled out.

The object: ordered partitions and the sampler family
A multi-token unmasking schedule is encoded as an ordered partition \(z = (z_1, \dots, z_d)\) of the index set: \(\bigcup_i z_i = I\), \(z_i \cap z_j = \emptyset\) for \(i \neq j\), where \(z_i \subseteq I\) is the set of positions unmasked at step \(i\) (padding with \(z_i = \emptyset\) allowed, so every schedule fits in exactly \(d\) slots). Write \(z_{<i} = (z_1, \dots, z_{i-1})\) and, for an ordered sub-partition \(s\), let \(x^s\) denote the tokens at indices \(I_s = \bigcup_j s_j\).
A sampler is a conditional distribution \(\phi(z_i \mid x^{z_{<i}}, z_{<i})\): given everything unmasked so far (both the values \(x^{z_{<i}}\) and the history of choices \(z_{<i}\)), it picks which set of positions to unmask next. WLOG \(\phi\) picks \(z_i = \emptyset\) only when everything is already unmasked. Running \(\phi\) with the learned conditionals defines a joint distribution over (sequence, schedule):
\[
p^{\phi}(x, z) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{d} \Bigg( \prod_{l \in z_i} p^\theta\big(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}}\big) \Bigg)\, \phi\big(z_i \mid x^{z_{<i}}, z_{<i}\big),
\tag{5}
\]
— the inner product over \(l \in z_i\) is precisely the “sample this step’s tokens independently” operation. The idealized reference process replaces the independent learned conditionals with the true joint conditional:
\[
q^{\phi}(x, z) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{d} q\big(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}\big)\, \phi\big(z_i \mid x^{z_{<i}}, z_{<i}\big).
\tag{6}
\]
Lemma (schedule-invariance of \(q^\phi\)). \(\displaystyle\sum_z q^{\phi}(x, z) = q(x)\) for every \(\phi\).
Proof. For any fixed partition \(z\), the chain rule gives \(\prod_i q(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}) = q(x)\) — the true conditionals compose to the true joint no matter how you group and order the coordinates. Hence \(q^{\phi}(x, z) = q(x) \prod_i \phi(z_i \mid x^{z_{<i}}, z_{<i})\), and summing the product of normalized conditionals \(\phi\) over all \(z\) (telescoping step by step, since each factor sums to 1 given its past) yields \(q(x)\). \(\blacksquare\)
This lemma is the family’s correctness guarantee and the justification for using a pretrained MDM with any new sampler: if \(p^\theta = q\) exactly, then by the same argument \(\sum_z p^{\phi}(x,z) = q(x)\) for every \(\phi\) — the zero-model-error optimum is shared across the entire family, so choosing \(\phi\) is purely a question of how errors compound, not of what the target is. Note also how expressive the family is: Top-\(k\) with any proxy, random-order unmasking, LLaDa’s semi-autoregressive block sampling, and EB-Sampler are all particular (deterministic) choices of \(\phi\).


Step 1: the ELBO-style bound
We care about the mismatch between the data distribution and what the sampler actually produces, \(p^{\phi}(x) = \sum_z p^{\phi}(x,z)\). The first move is standard but worth doing precisely. By the chain rule for KL divergence applied to the joint,
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q^{\phi}(x,z),\, p^{\phi}(x,z)\big)
= D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q^{\phi}(x),\, p^{\phi}(x)\big)
+ \mathbb{E}_{q(x)}\Big[ D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q^{\phi}(z \mid x),\, p^{\phi}(z \mid x)\big) \Big],
\]
and since the lemma gives \(q^{\phi}(x) = q(x)\) and the second term is non-negative,
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q(x),\, p^{\phi}(x)\big) \;\le\; D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q^{\phi}(x,z),\, p^{\phi}(x,z)\big).
\tag{10a}
\]
This is the same maneuver that produces the ELBO in a VAE — bound an intractable marginal divergence by a tractable joint one. The joint divergence now factorizes beautifully: plugging (5) and (6) into the log-ratio, the \(\phi\) factors cancel (they appear identically in both joints), leaving
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q^{\phi}(x,z),\, p^{\phi}(x,z)\big)
= \sum_{i=1}^{d} \mathbb{E}_{q^{\phi}}\Bigg[ \ln q\big(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}\big) - \sum_{l \in z_i} \ln p^\theta\big(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}}\big) \Bigg].
\tag{10b}
\]
Each summand compares the true joint conditional of step \(i\)’s tokens against the product of learned marginals actually sampled — the two ways one step can go wrong, still entangled.


Step 2: the exact error decomposition
Theorem (error decomposition, Eq. 7 of the paper).
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q^{\phi}, p^{\phi}\big)
= \sum_{i=1}^{d} \mathbb{E}_{q^{\phi}}\Bigg[
\underbrace{\sum_{l \in z_i} D_{\mathrm{KL}}\Big(q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}}),\, p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})\Big)}_{\textbf{model error}}
\;+\;
\underbrace{D_{\mathrm{KL}}\Big(q(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}),\, \textstyle\prod_{l \in z_i} q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})\Big)}_{\textbf{joint dependence error}}
\Bigg].
\]
Proof. Add and subtract \(\sum_{l \in z_i} \ln q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})\) inside each summand of (10b):
\[
\ln q\big(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}\big) - \sum_{l \in z_i} \ln p^\theta\big(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}}\big)
= \sum_{l \in z_i} \ln \frac{q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})}{p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})}
+ \ln \frac{q(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}})}{\prod_{l \in z_i} q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})}.
\tag{11}
\]
It remains to show each term’s expectation under \(q^{\phi}\) is the corresponding KL. Both reductions use the same two facts, so let me state them once:

(∗) Irrelevant-coordinate marginalization. The \(i\)-th summand depends only on \((z_{\le i}, x^{z_{\le i}})\), so the expectation over the full \(q^{\phi}(x, z)\) collapses to one over \(q^{\phi}(z_{\le i}, x^{z_{\le i}})\) — the future of the trajectory integrates out.
(∗∗) The reference process samples true conditionals. Under \(q^{\phi}\), conditioned on the visible prefix and the choice history, the newly revealed tokens are distributed as the data conditional: \[
q^{\phi}\big(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}, z_{\le i}\big) = q\big(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}\big).
\] This is by construction in (6) — and it is not trivial: it holds because \(\phi\) selects \(z_i\) using only \((x^{z_{<i}}, z_{<i})\), never peeking at masked values, so conditioning on the choice \(z_i\) adds no information about \(x^{z_i}\) beyond the visible tokens. This measurability restriction on \(\phi\) is where the family definition earns its keep.

Right term of (11) → joint dependence error. By (∗), then splitting the expectation over \(x^{z_i}\) via (∗∗):
\[
\mathbb{E}_{q^{\phi}}\!\left[ \ln \frac{q(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}})}{\prod_l q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})} \right]
= \mathbb{E}_{q^{\phi}(z_{\le i},\, x^{z_{<i}})}\!\left[ \sum_{x^{z_i}} q\big(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}\big) \ln \frac{q(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}})}{\prod_l q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})} \right]
= \mathbb{E}\Big[ D_{\mathrm{KL}}\Big(q(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}), \prod_l q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})\Big) \Big].
\]
Left term of (11) → model error. Same two steps, plus one more marginalization (∗∗∗): the \(l\)-th log-ratio depends only on \(x^l\), so summing \(q(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}})\) over the other coordinates of \(z_i\) leaves the marginal \(q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})\):
\[
\mathbb{E}_{q^{\phi}}\!\left[ \sum_{l \in z_i} \ln \frac{q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})}{p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})} \right]
= \mathbb{E}_{q^{\phi}(z_{\le i},\, x^{z_{<i}})}\!\left[ \sum_{l \in z_i} \sum_{x^l} q\big(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}}\big) \ln \frac{q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})}{p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})} \right],
\]
which is the expected sum of per-token KLs. \(\blacksquare\)
Pause on what this theorem buys. The two failure modes of parallel sampling — my conditionals are wrong and these tokens aren’t actually independent — are not just intuitions; they are orthogonal summands of an exact identity. Model error is fixed by the checkpoint and the visited contexts; the sampler cannot reduce it, only route around its worst spots (that’s what the error-proxy ordering attempts). Joint dependence error is entirely the sampler’s creation: it vanishes for one-token-per-step sampling (\(|z_i| = 1\) makes the joint equal the product, KL \(= 0\)) and is the only new error introduced by parallelism. A principled multi-token sampler must therefore (a) select low-model-error tokens and (b) budget the dependence term. EB-Sampler is (a) via the proxies, (b) via what comes next.


Step 3: bounding the dependence term by sum-minus-max of entropies
Proposition (Eq. 8 of the paper). For any realized context \(x^{z_{<i}}\), writing \(H(q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}}))\) for the entropy of the true conditional of coordinate \(l\),
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}\Big(q(x^{z_i} \mid x^{z_{<i}}),\, \prod_{l \in z_i} q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})\Big)
\;\le\; \sum_{l \in z_i} H\big(q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})\big) - \max_{l \in z_i} H\big(q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})\big).
\]
Proof. All entropies below are conditioned on the fixed realization \(x^{z_{<i}}\); abbreviate the joint variable \(X^{z_i}\) and its coordinates \(X^l\). Expand the KL against the product of the joint’s own marginals:
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}\Big(q(x^{z_i}),\, \prod_l q(x^l)\Big)
= \sum_{x^{z_i}} q(x^{z_i}) \ln q(x^{z_i}) \;-\; \sum_{l \in z_i} \sum_{x^{z_i}} q(x^{z_i}) \ln q(x^l)
= \sum_{l \in z_i} H(X^l) - H(X^{z_i}),
\]
where the cross term reduced coordinate-wise because \(\sum_{x^{z_i}} q(x^{z_i}) \ln q(x^l) = \sum_{x^l} q(x^l) \ln q(x^l) = -H(X^l)\). This quantity is the (conditional) total correlation of the step’s tokens. Now bound the joint entropy from below: for any \(l_0 \in z_i\), \(H(X^{z_i}) \ge H(X^{l_0})\) (a discrete joint carries at least as much entropy as any single coordinate, since \(H(X^{z_i}) = H(X^{l_0}) + H(X^{z_i \setminus l_0} \mid X^{l_0})\) and conditional entropy is non-negative). Taking \(l_0 = \arg\max_l H(X^l)\) gives \(H(X^{z_i}) \ge \max_l H(X^l)\), and substituting yields the claim. \(\blacksquare\)
Sanity checks that convinced me the bound is the right one and not merely a bound:

Two tokens. For \(|z_i| = 2\) the total correlation is the mutual information \(I(X^1; X^2)\) and sum-minus-max is \(\min(H_1, H_2)\) — so the proposition specializes to the classical \(I(X;Y) \le \min(H(X), H(Y))\), with equality iff one token is a deterministic function of the other. Tight, and achieved exactly in the “copied token” worst case.
The free rider, again. Any token with \(H = 0\) changes neither the sum-minus-max (adds 0 to the sum) nor the true total correlation (a deterministic coordinate is independent of everything). The budget statistic treats forced tokens as exactly free because they are.
Why not just the sum? \(\sum_l H_l\) alone also upper-bounds total correlation, but it charges you for unmasking even a single uncertain token — which introduces zero dependence error. Subtracting the max is what makes the bound (and the sampler) correctly refuse to penalize the one genuinely-being-decided token per step. The \(-\max\) in the algorithm is the \(H(X^{z_i}) \ge \max_l H_l\) step of the proof, no more and no less.



Step 4: the plug-in step — where exactness ends
The bound above is in terms of entropies of the true conditionals \(q\), which we don’t have. The paper’s final move: assume \(\phi\) selects only tokens where model error is negligible, \(p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}}) \approx q(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})\) for all \(l \in z_i\) — this is precisely what sorting by the error proxy is trying to arrange. Then (i) the model-error summand of the theorem is \(\approx 0\), and (ii) the entropies in the proposition can be evaluated under \(p^\theta\) instead of \(q\), giving the computable criterion (EB). The per-step error contribution is then \(\lesssim \gamma\).
Being precise about what’s rigorous and what isn’t — the part I most wanted to write down cleanly:

Exact: the decomposition theorem (an identity) and the entropy bound (an inequality in \(q\)-quantities). No approximation anywhere.
Heuristic, empirically supported: confidence/entropy/margin as proxies for local model error (Fact 1’s Figure 2 is the evidence; there is no bound connecting proxy rank to KL).
Approximation, uncontrolled but controllable in principle: swapping \(H(q(\cdot))\) for \(H(p^\theta(\cdot))\). Entropy is continuous in total variation (on a finite alphabet, \(|H(p) - H(q)| \le \delta \ln(K-1) + h_b(\delta)\) for \(\|p-q\|_{TV} = \delta\), by Fannes–Audenaert), and TV is controlled by the local model-error KL via Pinsker — so if the proxy really does pick low-KL tokens, the entropy swap is quantitatively safe. The paper leaves this chain implicit; it’s the one place where “approximately bounds” is doing unacknowledged work, and I’d have liked a corollary stating it.



Coda: when is the ELBO bound an equality?
Appendix A.1 closes a loop most sampling papers leave open: when does minimizing the tractable joint KL actually minimize the marginal KL we care about? Equality in (10a) holds iff \(q^{\phi}(z \mid x) = p^{\phi}(z \mid x)\). Computing both posteriors from the lemma and (5):
\[
q^{\phi}(z \mid x) = \prod_{i=1}^{d} \phi\big(z_i \mid x^{z_{<i}}, z_{<i}\big),
\qquad
p^{\phi}(z \mid x) = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{d} \prod_{l \in z_i} p^\theta(x^l \mid x^{z_{<i}})}{p^{\phi}(x)} \, q^{\phi}(z \mid x).
\]
The prefactor is the order-dependence of the learned conditionals: for the true \(q\) the product over any schedule is \(q(x)\) (chain rule), but for a learned \(p^\theta\) different schedules compose to different joints — so the prefactor is generically \(\neq 1\) and the bound is strict, even for one-token-per-step sampling. The exception: deterministic \(\phi\) (which covers Top-\(k\) with deterministic tie-breaking, and EB-Sampler). Then \(z = z_\phi(x)\) is a function of the sample, both posteriors are the same point mass, and
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q(x), p^{\phi}(x)\big) = D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q^{\phi}(x,z), p^{\phi}(x,z)\big)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathbb{E}_q\big[\ln p^{\phi}(x)\big] = \mathbb{E}_{q^{\phi}}\big[\ln p^{\phi}(x, z_\phi(x))\big].
\]
So for the samplers people actually run, the error decomposition is not a loose surrogate — it is the likelihood objective, exactly. I find this a quietly important paragraph: it says the design target “minimize model error routing + dependence budget” is the true target, not an ELBO-shaped shadow of it.

title: [Paper Review] Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking
text:
Experiments: the honest 2–3×
Code and math (LLaDa 8B, Dream 7B; HumanEval, MBPP, GSM8K, MATH). Across all eight model×benchmark pairs and both proxies, EB-Sampler sits on or above the Top-\(k\) Pareto frontier of accuracy vs. NFE, with 2–4× fewer function evaluations than Top-1 at matched accuracy. The comparison is apples-to-apples: same checkpoints, same zero-temperature setting, the only change is the four lines.
The measurement methodology is a contribution in itself. MDM evaluation conventionally generates all max_gen_len tokens (512 for MBPP) even when the answer needs ~50 — so naive NFE counts reward samplers for quickly generating tokens that get thrown away. The authors add a generate_until stopping logic (answer-complete when the stop phrase appears and everything before it is unmasked), and then notice a further bias: at \(\gamma\) small the model happily continues unmasking past the stop phrase. Their Table 1 on MBPP with Dream 7B is the paper in microcosm: EB at \(\gamma = 0.1\) reads as a 6.0× speedup against full-length generation, 4.0× under generate_until, and 3.05× under generate_until + semi-AR block generation — and the authors explicitly decline to claim the 6×, calling 2–3× the honest estimate (≈2.4 tokens per forward pass at equal pass@1). A results section that argues its own headline number down earns trust for everything else. It also surfaces a real open problem: MDMs burn compute on tokens nobody will read, and no current sampler fully avoids it.
Logic puzzles (6M-parameter DiT, 10×10 DFS mazes and 9×9 Sudoku). The regime where the forced-cascade intuition is most literal. All error proxies perform near-identically; the difference is the shape of the degradation curve. Top-\(k\) decays steadily as \(k\) grows; EB-Sampler holds near-full accuracy down to ~10–15 NFE on Sudoku (89-token sequences — roughly 6–9 tokens per pass) and to under ~5 NFE on mazes before a sharp cliff. The cliff is exactly what the theory predicts: dependence in a constraint-satisfaction problem is not smoothly distributed — either your budget respects the constraint graph’s forced structure or it starts guessing coupled cells independently, and there is no graceful middle.

title: [Paper Review] Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking
text:
My take
The headline number is modest as 2025 acceleration papers go — speculative decoding bought comparable factors for ARMs years earlier — but I think this is the right paper to study in the MDM-efficiency line, for two reasons.
First, it’s the cleanest instance I know of a pattern worth internalizing: an exact error decomposition, then one term attacked per mechanism. The identity “total KL = model error + joint dependence error” does all the conceptual work. Once you have it, the algorithm designs itself: proxies route around the term you can’t reduce; the entropy budget caps the term you create. Even the algorithm’s oddest-looking detail — subtracting the max entropy — turns out to be a single inequality in the proof (\(H_{\text{joint}} \ge \max_l H_l\)) surfacing in the code. Compare Top-\(k\), which in this framing is EB-Sampler with the budget replaced by “cardinality \(= k\)” — a constraint on a quantity the error decomposition never mentions. Of course it traces a worse Pareto curve; it’s rationing the wrong resource.
Second, the family formalism is more valuable than the sampler. Equations (5)–(6) give a clean design space — measurable unmasking policies \(\phi\) over ordered partitions — with a correctness lemma (any \(\phi\) is exact at zero model error), a shared objective (the decomposition), and an equality result for the deterministic members. LLaDa’s semi-AR sampling, all the Top-\(k\) variants, and EB are points in this space; the paper’s own suggested future work (learn \(\phi\) by optimizing the KL bound; extend to remasking) reads as a research agenda for the space rather than an add-on. My guess is the framework gets cited longer than the specific budget rule.
The honest gaps, both acknowledged to differing degrees: the proxy step (rank by confidence/entropy/margin ⇒ low model error) is where the whole guarantee chain passes through an empirical observation, and a token that is confidently wrong — low entropy, high KL — sails under the budget at zero charge; the bound is only as good as the model’s calibration, and the paper doesn’t probe miscalibration at all. And the framework deliberately excludes remasking, so an early parallel mistake is permanent — the predictor-corrector line (e.g. ReMDM) is solving the complementary problem, and the natural combination (EB for width, remasking for repair) is unexplored. Neither gap undermines the paper; both are exactly the kind of open ends a good formalism is supposed to expose.

title: [Paper Review] Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
text: Paper: Michael Janner*, Yilun Du*, Joshua B. Tenenbaum, Sergey Levine. Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis. ICML 2022. arXiv:2205.09991 · PMLR · Project page · Code

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
text:
TL;DR
Model-based RL usually means: learn a one-step dynamics model, then hand it to a classical trajectory optimizer. In practice the optimizer exploits the model’s errors, producing adversarial plans rather than optimal ones. Diffuser dissolves the model/planner boundary: train a denoising diffusion model directly over entire trajectories — a 2D array of states and actions — so that planning becomes almost identical to sampling. A plan is not rolled out step by step; it emerges whole, refined from noise over the denoising iterations.
Two conditioning mechanisms make the samples useful for control, both borrowed from image diffusion: rewards enter as classifier-guided sampling (gradients of a learned return model nudge the denoising mean), and constraints — most importantly the initial and final states of the trajectory — enter as inpainting: the constrained entries of the array are simply overwritten with their fixed values after every denoising step, exactly like known pixels in image inpainting. That second mechanism is the answer to a question I had when reading: can you fix where a generated trajectory starts and ends? Here it is not a trick bolted on afterwards — it is how every plan is conditioned on the current state, and how goal-reaching is solved with zero reward signal. On the sparse-reward Maze2D benchmark, this start/goal inpainting alone scores 119.5 average (vs 47.0 for the best model-free baseline), and the same trained model transfers to randomized goals without any retraining — you just clamp a different terminal state.

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
text:
Why this problem matters
Trajectory optimization is the cleanest story in control: write down the objective, let the optimizer find the actions. But it needs ground-truth dynamics \(s_{t+1} = f(s_t, a_t)\). The learning-based workaround — approximate \(f\) with a neural network, plug it into the optimizer — fails in a characteristic way: powerful optimizers treat model error as opportunity, steering into regions where the learned model hallucinates high reward. So practical model-based RL retreated to weak gradient-free planners, or abandoned planning for value functions.
Diffuser’s proposal: if the planner is going to be tightly coupled to the model anyway, design the model around the planning problem. Predict full trajectories non-autoregressively (no compounding one-step error), model actions jointly with states (a plan needs both), stay reward-agnostic (one model, many tasks), and make the sampling procedure itself the planner.

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
section: The diffusion model: planning as sampling
text:
The diffusion model: planning as sampling
Trajectories as arrays. Diffuser represents a plan as a two-dimensional array — one column per planning timestep, states stacked on actions:
\[
\tau \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
s_0 & s_1 & \cdots & s_T \\
a_0 & a_1 & \cdots & a_T
\end{bmatrix}
\]
The model learns \(p_\theta(\tau)\) with a standard diffusion setup (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020): a forward process corrupts trajectories with Gaussian noise over diffusion steps \(i = 1,\dots,N\), and a learned reverse process \(p_\theta(\tau^{i-1} \mid \tau^i)\) denoises them back. Training is the usual simplified \(\epsilon\)-prediction objective \(\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{i,\epsilon,\tau^0}\big[\lVert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\tau^i, i)\rVert^2\big]\) with a cosine noise schedule. Notation to keep straight: superscripts are diffusion time (noise level), subscripts are planning time.
Why generation must be non-autoregressive. This is my favorite conceptual point in the paper. Dynamics prediction is causal — the present follows from the past — but decision-making is anti-causal: what I should do now depends on where I want to end up. Formally, goal-conditioned inference \(p(s_1 \mid s_0, s_T)\) conditions on the future. A left-to-right autoregressive model structurally cannot do this; a diffusion model that generates all timesteps concurrently can. This is precisely the property that makes terminal-state conditioning possible at all — information from the clamped end of the trajectory propagates backwards through planning time during denoising.
Architecture: local steps, global coherence. The denoiser is a U-Net of 1D temporal convolutions — the image-diffusion U-Net with spatial convolutions swapped for convolutions along the horizon axis. Each denoising step sees only a local temporal receptive field, so a single step can only enforce local consistency (do adjacent transitions look dynamically plausible?). Global coherence — a path that actually connects start to goal — emerges from composing many local refinements. Two nice consequences: the model is fully convolutional in time, so the planning horizon is set by the width of the input noise, not the architecture (variable-length plans for free); and the local-consistency bias lets Diffuser stitch in-distribution trajectory snippets into novel composite plans (trained only on straight-line trajectories, it generalizes to v-shaped ones — Figure 3b).






Figure 2: Figure 3 of the paper: properties of diffusion planners. (a) Long-horizon sparse-reward planning where shooting methods fail. (b) Temporal compositionality: trained only on straight-line trajectories, Diffuser stitches them into novel v-shaped plans. (c) Variable-length plans via the input-noise dimensionality. (d) Task compositionality: a new reward unseen in training, applied as guidance at sampling time. ⦿ is the start state, ★ the goal. (Janner et al., 2022)

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
section: Conditioning I: generating the initial and terminal states by inpainting
text:
Conditioning I: generating the initial and terminal states by inpainting
Sampling from \(p_\theta(\tau)\) gives plausible trajectories from anywhere to anywhere. Planning needs trajectories that satisfy conditions. Diffuser frames all conditioning as sampling from a perturbed distribution
\[
\tilde{p}_\theta(\tau) \;\propto\; p_\theta(\tau)\, h(\tau),
\]
where \(h(\tau)\) carries everything task-specific — and because the dynamics knowledge lives entirely in \(p_\theta\), one trained model serves every task in the environment.
The case I want to dwell on — and the reason I picked up this paper — is hard state constraints: fixing where the trajectory begins and ends. Section 3.3 observes that with the 2D-array trajectory representation, this is literally the inpainting problem from image diffusion: constrained states play the role of observed pixels, and the model must fill in everything else consistently. The perturbation function is a Dirac delta on the constrained entries — for a constraint \(c_t\) at planning time \(t\):
\[
h(\tau) \;=\; \delta_{c_t}(s_0, a_0, \dots, s_T, a_T) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
+\infty & \text{if } c_t = s_t \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]
The implementation is disarmingly simple. At every denoising step \(i \in \{N, \dots, 1\}\):

sample \(\tau^{i-1} \sim p_\theta(\tau^{i-1} \mid \tau^i)\) as usual;
overwrite the constrained entries with their fixed values — for start/goal conditioning, \(\tau_{s_0} \leftarrow s_{\text{current}}\) and \(\tau_{s_T} \leftarrow s_{\text{goal}}\).

Because the clamp is re-imposed after every step, the denoiser is repeatedly forced to reconcile the free middle of the trajectory with both fixed endpoints. The anti-causal, bidirectional receptive field is what lets the goal at \(t{=}T\) shape the actions at \(t{=}0\) — over the denoising iterations, boundary information diffuses inward through planning time until the inpainted middle is a dynamically consistent path connecting the two. Figure 4 makes this visual and is, for me, the most striking figure of the paper: red point-clouds of noise crystallize into a single path pinned at ⦿ and ★.






Figure 3: Figure 4 of the paper: planning as inpainting in Maze2D. The start ⦿ and goal ★ states are clamped; denoising “inpaints” the intermediate states into a feasible path. (Janner et al., 2022)



Three things worth underlining about this mechanism:

It is universal, not optional. Even pure reward-maximization runs use it — every plan must begin at the current state, so \(\tau_{s_0} \leftarrow s\) is line 10 of the planning algorithm, executed unconditionally. Terminal-state clamping is the same line applied at the other end. Initial and termination conditions are first-class citizens of the sampler.
It generalizes beyond endpoints. Nothing is special about \(t=0\) and \(t=T\): any subset of the array can be clamped — intermediate waypoints, even action dimensions (the paper’s definition for action constraints is identical). You could pin a mid-trajectory checkpoint just as easily.
It is a hard constraint, training-free, and retarget-able. No goal-conditioned training, no reward shaping. The Multi2D experiment is the payoff: the single-task Maze2D model is reused with randomized goals by changing only the clamped value of \(\tau_{s_T}\), and performance doesn’t move (129.4 vs 119.5). The caveat is the flip side of the same coin: clamping guarantees the endpoints appear, but the inpainted middle is only as feasible as the training distribution — clamp an endpoint pair the data never connects, and the model must extrapolate.

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
section: Conditioning II: reward as classifier guidance
text:
Conditioning II: reward as classifier guidance
For reward maximization, Diffuser ports classifier-guided sampling through the control-as-inference lens. Let \(\mathcal{O}_t\) be the optimality variable with \(p(\mathcal{O}_t = 1) = \exp(r(s_t, a_t))\); setting \(h(\tau) = p(\mathcal{O}_{1:T} \mid \tau)\) turns RL into conditional sampling. When the guidance term is smooth, the guided reverse step is Gaussian with a shifted mean:
\[
p_\theta(\tau^{i-1} \mid \tau^i, \mathcal{O}_{1:T}) \;\approx\; \mathcal{N}\!\big(\tau^{i-1};\; \mu + \alpha\Sigma\,\nabla\mathcal{J}(\mu),\; \Sigma^i\big)
\]
where \(\mathcal{J}_\phi\) is a separately trained return predictor on noisy trajectories, standing in for the image-domain classifier. Guidance and inpainting compose in the same loop — shift the mean by \(\alpha\Sigma\nabla\mathcal{J}\), sample, clamp \(\tau_{s_0}\), repeat — and multiple guides can be added (task compositionality, Figure 3d: a reward function never seen during training steers the same model at test time). Execution is receding-horizon: plan, execute the first action, observe, replan.

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
text:
Experiments
Long-horizon sparse reward (Maze2D / Multi2D). Reward is 1 only at the goal, hundreds of steps away — a credit-assignment nightmare for model-free RL. Diffuser plans by pure start/goal inpainting (no reward guidance at all) and scores 113.9–123.0 across maze sizes (>100 = better than the reference expert), vs 47.0 average for IQL and worse for CQL and MPPI — the latter with ground-truth dynamics, which neatly isolates planning difficulty from model error. In the multi-goal variant, IQL (with hindsight relabeling) drops to 16.9 while Diffuser holds at 129.4, unchanged, because retargeting is just a different clamp.
Test-time flexibility (block stacking). One model trained on PDDLStream demonstrations handles three tasks by swapping \(h(\tau)\): unconditional stacking (no perturbation), conditional stacking and rearrangement (a final-state-matching guide composed with an end-effector contact constraint). Diffuser averages 54.4 where goal-conditioned BCQ and CQL score 0.0 — the conditional settings simply break the model-free baselines.
Offline locomotion (D4RL). On the D4RL MuJoCo suite with reward guidance, Diffuser averages 77.5 — on par with the best purpose-built single-task methods (CQL 77.6, IQL 77.0, Trajectory Transformer 78.9, beating Decision Transformer 74.7). A telling ablation: using Diffuser merely as a dynamics model inside MPPI performs no better than random — the value is in the coupled model-planner, not in prediction accuracy.
Speed, the honest caveat. Iterative denoising per plan is slow. Warm-starting helps a lot: re-noise the previous timestep’s plan a few steps and re-denoise, cutting the budget ~10× with only a modest score drop (Figure 7). Still, this is 2022-era diffusion sampling — slow relative to a policy forward pass.

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
text:
Strengths

A genuinely new abstraction for planning. “Sampling = planning” is not a slogan here — the planner is the sampler plus a perturbation function. This eliminates the model-exploitation failure mode by construction: there is no outer-loop optimizer to adversarially probe the model.
Conditioning by inpainting is elegant and exactly right for goal-reaching. Hard endpoint constraints, zero retraining, task retargeting by changing one clamped value, and it is the Maze2D result — the paper’s biggest margin comes from its simplest mechanism.
The anti-causality argument. The observation that control is anti-causal while autoregressive models are causal is a crisp, transferable insight that justifies the whole non-autoregressive design — and predicts exactly which problems (goal-conditioned, sparse-reward, long-horizon) diffusion planners should win.
Compositionality demonstrated, not just claimed. Trajectory stitching (3b), variable horizons (3c), unseen rewards (3d), and constraint composition in block stacking are each backed by an experiment.

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
text:
Weaknesses / open questions

Feasibility of inpainted plans is distributional, not guaranteed. Clamping makes the endpoints appear; nothing certifies the middle respects dynamics or obstacles when conditions leave the data manifold. Plans are also executed open-loop in Maze2D — fine there, fragile in general.
Reward guidance is the weaker of the two mechanisms. On D4RL, Diffuser only matches model-free baselines, and \(\mathcal{J}_\phi\) must be trained per reward on noisy trajectories — a somewhat awkward object. Later work (classifier-free variants like Decision Diffuser) largely replaced it.
Sampling cost. Even warm-started, denoising-per-decision is orders of magnitude more expensive than a policy. The paper is upfront about this; it remains the main deployment barrier for this family.
Low-dimensional states only. Everything here is proprioceptive/planar state; image-based control is untouched (and the temporal-U-Net-over-state-vectors design doesn’t obviously transfer).

title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
text:
My take
Reading this in 2026, Diffuser is clearly a lineage-starting paper — diffusion policies, guided trajectory generation, and half of today’s robot-learning stacks trace back to the two conditioning mechanisms in Section 3. What struck me most on a close read is the asymmetry between them: the reward-guidance half (Section 3.2) is the part the title advertises, but the inpainting half (Section 3.3) is the part that wins the experiments and that survived best in follow-up work. Fixing the initial condition is what makes any of it a planner; fixing the terminal condition is what makes it a goal-reaching planner with free task transfer. And the mechanism is one line: sample, clamp, repeat. The deeper enabler — easy to miss — is the anti-causal generation order: only a model that generates all timesteps jointly can let a constraint at \(t{=}T\) reshape the action at \(t{=}0\).
The through-line to my earlier reviews is inference-time steering of a frozen diffusion model: SGF steers away from unsafe regions with a soft guidance force and proves when to apply it; Diffuser steers toward conditions with both a soft force (reward gradients) and a hard clamp (inpainting). The hard-constraint end of that spectrum is exactly where the safety line and the planning line meet — SafeDiffuser later put control barrier functions inside Diffuser’s denoising steps to certify constraint satisfaction, closing the loop between these two literatures.
Verdict: the founding paper of diffusion-based planning, and still the cleanest exposition of the two conditioning mechanisms everything since builds on. If you only read one section, read 3.3 — the inpainting trick is the highest insight-per-equation in the paper.

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
text: Paper: Mingyu Kim, Young-Heon Kim, Mijung Park. Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation. ICLR 2026 (Oral). arXiv:2603.13300 · OpenReview · Code

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
text:
TL;DR
How do you make a diffusion or flow model generate safe content without retraining it? Recent training-free answers — Shielded Diffusion (SPELL) and Safe Denoiser — both push samples away from a set of known-unsafe examples during denoising, but each with its own ad-hoc force and an empirically hand-tuned schedule.
This paper, Safety-Guided Flow (SGF), gives that whole family a single principled foundation. It defines an energy landscape over the sample space using the Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) to a negation set of unsafe examples, and steers generation uphill on that energy — away from everything unsafe. Both SPELL and Safe Denoiser fall out as special cases of this one gradient field. It then uses control barrier function (CBF) analysis to prove what previous work only observed empirically: safety guidance is only effective inside a critical time window early in denoising, and should be switched off afterwards. Plugged into SAFREE on Stable Diffusion, SGF drops the nudity attack success rate on Ring-A-Bell from 27.8% to 5.1% with essentially no image-quality cost, and the same mechanism mitigates memorization (95th-percentile train similarity 0.437 → 0.328 while FID improves).
If you read my review of the Safety-Aware Denoiser (SAD) — SAD’s text-diffusion guidance is built directly on the Safe Denoiser this paper generalizes, and SAD’s “early time window” heuristic is exactly what SGF proves. This is the theory piece of that research line.

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
text:
Why this problem matters
Diffusion and flow models are deployed in places where unsafe outputs have real consequences — NSFW or biased images, regurgitated private training data, unsafe robot trajectories. Two research communities converged on the same instinct from opposite directions:

Robot planning uses control barrier functions to keep generated trajectories out of geometrically-defined obstacle regions at every denoising step — rigorous, but built for low-dimensional states and engineered constraints, with no probabilistic view of data.
Image generation uses data-driven negative guidance (SPELL’s radial repulsion, Safe Denoiser’s denoiser decomposition) — practical and training-free, but heuristic: neither says when guidance is actually necessary, and always-on guidance is known to wreck sample quality.

SGF’s contribution is to merge the two: a probabilistic repulsive force (from the image line) with a formal reach-avoid analysis of its timing (from the control line).

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
section: The core mechanism: how SGF generates safe content
text:
The core mechanism: how SGF generates safe content
This is the heart of the paper, so let me walk through the full pipeline of how a sample ends up safe.
Step 1 — Define “unsafe” by example, not by rule. You curate a negation set \(\mathcal{D}^- = \{y_i\}_{i=1}^N\) of concrete unsafe examples: 515 nudity images from I2P, copyrighted works, or the training set itself (for memorization). No classifier, no fine-tuning, no text rules — unsafety is defined extensionally, by what you show it.
Step 2 — Turn the negation set into an energy landscape. SGF defines a potential at the current sample \(x_t\) as the squared MMD between a point mass at \(x_t\) and the negation set, under an RBF kernel \(k_\sigma\):
\[
E(x_t) \equiv \widehat{\mathrm{MMD}}^2_{k_\sigma}\!\left(\{x_t\}, \mathcal{D}^-\right)
\]
This energy is low when \(x_t\) resembles unsafe examples and high when it is far from all of them. “Safe” is literally the high-energy region of this landscape.
Step 3 — Steer generation uphill. The flow-matching ODE gets one extra term:
\[
\dot{x}_t = f_\theta(x_t, t) + \lambda(t)\, \nabla_x E(x_t)
\]
The gradient has a beautifully interpretable closed form:
\[
\nabla_{x_t} E \propto \; x_t - \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i(x_t)\, y_i, \qquad w_i(x_t) \propto k_\sigma(x_t, y_i)
\]
That is: compute a kernel-weighted average of the unsafe examples nearest to where the sample is currently heading, and push directly away from it. Samples drifting toward unsafe territory feel a strong shove; samples already far away feel almost nothing (the RBF weights vanish). Safety is enforced during generation — the trajectory itself is bent around unsafe regions, rather than unsafe outputs being caught after the fact.
Step 4 — Apply the force early, then turn it off. The guidance schedule \(\lambda(t)\) is strong only during a critical window at the start of denoising and decays to zero afterwards. Early steps decide the coarse semantics of the output (is this image nude or not?); late steps only refine details. Push early and you cheaply redirect the sample to a safe mode; push late and you can no longer change the semantics — you just smear the details and degrade quality.
Why this is safe by construction, not by luck. Section 4.4 formalizes step 4 with control barrier functions. Model the safe set as \(S = \{h \geq 0\}\) for a barrier function \(h\), and assume the MMD gradient aligns with \(\nabla h\) near the safe/unsafe boundary. Theorem 2 then gives a sufficient condition for the sample to reach a \(\delta\)-margin inside the safe set by deadline \(s_c\) — and because the weighting term in that condition decays over time, moving a fixed guidance budget earlier strictly improves the safety bound. After the deadline, if the unguided flow keeps the safe set invariant (reasonable near the end of denoising, when the drift only refines details), guidance can be set to zero with safety preserved and fidelity improved. In short: earlier is provably better, and off-at-the-end is provably fine.






Figure 2: Figure 2 of the paper: the mechanism on a 2D toy flow. The learned velocity field (arrows) is combined with the MMD-gradient repulsion away from the negative data (orange). Full-time guidance (c) distorts modes near the unsafe set; early-stopped guidance (d) both avoids the unsafe region and matches the target distribution better (W² 0.937 vs 1.009). (Kim et al., 2026)

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
section: Unification: SPELL and Safe Denoiser are the same force
text:
Unification: SPELL and Safe Denoiser are the same force
The “unified framework” claim is made precise in two propositions:

Proposition 1 (Safe Denoiser): evaluating the MMD gradient at the predicted clean sample \(z_t = \mathbb{E}[x_0 \mid x_t]\) with a fixed bandwidth recovers Safe Denoiser’s weighted-kernel repellency, up to a positive scale. Safe Denoiser’s softmax weighting over unsafe references is the MMD gradient’s kernel weighting.
Proposition 2 (SPELL): SPELL’s thresholded radial push of radius \(r\) matches the single-point Gaussian MMD force at any prescribed distance, via an explicit radius–bandwidth correspondence (closed form through the Lambert \(W\) function). SPELL is a radius-thresholded instance of the same gradient field.

So two methods published as unrelated mechanisms are one repulsive field with different kernel/threshold choices — and their shared empirical quirk (“guidance must start strong and fade”) is now a theorem rather than folklore.

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
text:
Experiments
All on text-to-image Stable Diffusion variants, with the same negation sets as prior work for fairness:
Safe generation against adversarial nudity prompts (Ring-A-Bell, UnlearnDiff, MMA-Diffusion; NudeNet as judge). SGF is a plug-in on top of negative-prompt methods. On SAFREE, swapping Safe Denoiser for SGF cuts ASR from 12.7% → 5.1% (Ring-A-Bell), 20.7% → 16.4% (UnlearnDiff), 46.9% → 42.3% (MMA-Diffusion) — versus 27.8/35.3/60.1% for SAFREE alone and 79.7/80.9/96.2% for raw SD-v1.4. COCO image quality barely moves (FID 23.7, CLIP 30.4). Notably, the training-free stack beats the fine-tuned unlearning baselines (ESD, RECE) on the safety–quality Pareto front.






Figure 3: Safe generations from SAFREE + SGF (Figure G.5 of the paper) on a suggestive lingerie-and-candlelight prompt where previous methods fail. Each panel varies the negative-guidance time window: with a short early window ([1.0, 0.8]) a figure still appears, and as the window covers more of the critical early phase the model resolves the same prompt into pure candlelit still-life scenes — unsafe semantics steered away while the contextual meaning of the prompt (soft candlelight) is preserved. (Kim et al., 2026)



Memorization (SD-v2.1 deliberately overfit on ImageNette). Using the training images themselves as the negation set, SGF shifts the generated-to-train similarity distribution down: 95th-percentile similarity drops 0.437 → 0.328 (−24.7%), and with early stopping FID actually improves from 41.19 → 32.44 — pushing away from memorized modes apparently frees the model to generate more diverse, higher-quality samples.
Diversity (class-to-image on ImageNet, SD-v3). Against SPELL: at matched settings, SGF with early stop keeps FID at 31.8 (vs SPELL’s 48.5 at λ=1.0) while still improving Vendi diversity over the baseline. Early stopping is what rescues the quality.
The ablation that proves the point (Figure 4). Across three scheduling strategies — equal per-step strength, equal total budget \(\int \lambda(t)dt\), and a shifted fixed-length window — the lowest ASR always comes from windows at the earliest denoising steps (\([1.0, 0.8]\) or \([1.0, 0.6]\)). Even with the total guidance budget held constant, spending it late is strictly worse. This is exactly what Theorem 2 predicts.
Cost. On SAFREE, SGF adds 0.10s/image with N=515 negatives (baseline 4.22s) — the guidance is cheaper than the safety framework it plugs into.

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
text:
Strengths

It answers the question the prior methods dodged: when. Every negative-guidance paper (including SAD in text) hand-tunes a time window. SGF derives the critical window from a reach-avoid argument and validates it with a budget-controlled ablation. “Earlier is better” is now a design principle with a proof sketch behind it, not a hyperparameter accident.
Genuine unification. The two propositions aren’t decorative — they show one gradient field underlies methods from separate papers, which means improvements to the potential (kernel choice, bandwidth adaptation) propagate to the whole family.
Safety without a safety tax. The consistent finding across all three tasks is that early-stopped guidance improves safety while preserving — sometimes improving — quality and diversity. The memorization result (safety metric down 25%, FID down 21%) is the standout.
Composability. Like SAD in the text domain, SGF is a plug-in: it stacks on SLD and SAFREE and improves both, rather than competing with them.

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
text:
Weaknesses / open questions

The alignment assumption does heavy lifting. Theorem 2 assumes the MMD gradient aligns with the ideal barrier field near the safe/unsafe boundary (\(\nabla h \cdot \nabla E \geq \mu\)), and that the base drift is weak there. The authors acknowledge this is strong; if the negation set poorly covers the boundary of the true unsafe region, the alignment can fail exactly where it matters. Quantifying guidance mismatch is left to future work.
Same blind spot as all reference-based methods. Unsafety is defined by kernel proximity to finitely many examples in pixel/feature space. Semantically unsafe content that is far from every reference — a novel unsafe composition, a style transfer of an unsafe concept — feels no repulsion. The 42.3% residual ASR on MMA-Diffusion hints at this ceiling.
Bandwidth sensitivity is under-discussed. The RBF bandwidth \(\sigma\) controls the reach of the repulsion, and the adaptive procedure (pairwise-distance sorting) is both the cost driver at large N and a place where the theory-practice gap could hide. A sensitivity study would strengthen the claims.
Scope of the safety evaluation. Safety here means nudity + memorization on Stable Diffusion. Broader harm categories (violence, bias) and modern larger backbones are untested — though the mechanism is generic.

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
text:
My take
If SAD (my previous review) was the “apply it” paper, this is the “understand it” paper — and reading them together is more valuable than either alone. What I like most is that SGF converts the single most mysterious knob in this whole line of work — the guidance time window — from folklore into a theorem, and then shows in a controlled-budget ablation that the theorem’s prediction holds. That’s the right shape for a safety paper: the mechanism for generating safe content is not “we filter bad outputs” but the sampling dynamics themselves are bent away from unsafe regions, at the moment when the sample’s coarse semantics are being decided, and left alone afterwards. Safety becomes a property of the trajectory, not a patch on the output.
The unification also quietly raises the standard for future work: a new negative-guidance method now needs to either be a new potential (not just a new force heuristic) or improve the schedule beyond “early and decaying.” And the open problems are well-defined — semantic kernels for the blind-spot issue, and relaxing the boundary-alignment assumption, which is exactly the gap between “provably safe under assumptions” and “provably safe.”
Verdict: the theoretical backbone of training-free safe generation — read it to understand why early negative guidance works, then read SAD to see it deployed on text diffusion.

title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
text:
Peer review at a glance
SGF was accepted as an Oral at ICLR 2026 — the tier reserved for a small fraction of accepted papers — after an unusually divisive review round: ratings of 4/6/6/10. Scores via Paper Copilot, review text via a public mirror of the OpenReview data (the forum itself now requires sign-in):

What the reviewers raised — and how the authors answered
The rebuttal threads themselves aren’t in the public mirror, so the “answers” below are what’s verifiable in the camera-ready paper plus the outcome; where I’m inferring, I say so.
The make-or-break objection (Reviewer UsAs, rating 4). The skeptic’s central challenge: “wouldn’t standard gradient guidance with the control-barrier function work just as well? … If the authors can successfully argue why MMD (or really any probability distance) is more useful for the end goal of safe generation than just using standard gradient guidance with the control-barrier function I will raise my score, otherwise I will retain a reject.” In other words: if you have \(h\), why bother with MMD? The answer implicit in the paper’s own setup — and presumably the rebuttal’s core — is that \(h\) is not available in data-driven safety: the unsafe region is defined only by examples, and the barrier function exists purely as an analysis device (Section 4.4 introduces it “only for theoretical clarity”). The MMD potential is the thing you can actually compute from a negation set; Assumption 1 is precisely the bridge asserting its gradient behaves like \(\nabla h\) near the boundary. Whatever the authors argued, it worked: the paper went from this reviewer’s reject to an Oral. Notably, the camera-ready’s limitation paragraph cites exactly the two theory works this reviewer demanded engagement with (D-Flow and Blasingame & Liu) and names “quantifying guidance mismatch” as future work — a visible trace of the exchange.
“Isn’t the window still heuristic?” (also UsAs). How do you pick \(s_c\) — isn’t this Safe Denoiser’s hand-tuned interval all over again? The paper’s answer: Theorem 2 doesn’t hand you \(s_c\) in closed form, but it converts the choice from a free two-parameter schedule into a one-directional rule (earlier strictly dominates at fixed budget), and the equal-budget ablation (Figure 4b) validates that rule empirically. Partial credit is fair here — my weaknesses section makes a related point.
Compute cost of the MMD gradient (also UsAs). How expensive is this as \(\mathcal{D}^-\) grows? The camera-ready answers with measurements: Table 4 shows +0.10s/image at \(N{=}515\) and +0.48s at \(N{=}3{,}200\) on top of a 4.22s SAFREE baseline, with the adaptive-bandwidth procedure (Appendix D.1) identified as the cost driver.
“Theory without pragmatic novelty” (Reviewer TunD, rating 6). “The resulting parametric form of the guidance model itself is effectively identical to SafeDenoiser… the critical time window theory provides why a certain stopping parameter is better, but this can be empirically chosen without theory.” This is the honest deflationary read, and the paper largely accepts it — its contribution is explanatory unification, not a new mechanism. The counterargument (which I find persuasive) is the equal-budget ablation: knowing why early guidance wins tells you how to spend a fixed guidance budget, which pure empiricism only discovers by sweeping.
Assumption 1(b) and the non-monotonic ablation (Reviewer ATH6, rating 6). Two sharp technical questions: how does the weak-base-drift assumption hold away from the final time, and why does ASR dip then rise across shifted windows when Theorem 2 suggests monotonicity? The paper’s justification for Assumption 1(b) (drift reaching the unsafe region implies near-final time, where the denoiser force is small) is stated but acknowledged as strong — this became the paper’s own listed limitation, and it’s my weaknesses #1.
The champion (Reviewer h63Q, rating 10, confidence 5) called it “outstanding theoretical contribution… the first formal safety guarantees for generative flows” with “empirical alignment with theory.” Even this review’s mild criticisms converge with the others: discuss the weak-drift assumption’s generality, and extend beyond visual safety to semantic concepts like copyright and bias — the same blind spot I flag in weaknesses #2 and #4.
What I take from the round: all four reviewers, from reject to rare 10, agreed on the same pressure points — the strength of Assumption 1 and the question of what the theory buys in practice. The disagreement was about how much those matter relative to the unification. The area chairs’ Oral decision is a strong endorsement of the “conceptual contribution first” view.
